A couple ways you can fix this.
The first way you could fix this is use something that is sensitive to position like an R&N.
And an R&N looks at which words came before, which words came after and stuff like that.
So that would solve your problem.
But the whole point of transformers or attention is all you need is to not use R&Ns.
So we need another way to fix this problem.
So the way this is fixed is using something called positional encodings.
And so positional encodings add another embedding that's based on the word position.
And so in addition to having something that's based on the word identity, you have another
embedding that's based on the position.
So then the word big appears in position two would be embedding a big plus embedding
a position two.
And the word big that appears over here would be the embedding a big and then the embedding
a position eight, for example.

But the biggest disadvantage is it makes it impossible to extrapolate to longer sequences

So going back to the question, there's a distinction between absolute versus relative
positional encodings.
And absolute positional encodings are like what I said before.
They're basically positional encodings where you add in a, you specifically add in an
encoding at each position.
But you don't consider whether like one query vector is close to a key vector or far away
from a key vector or anything like that.
You don't consider that directly.
On the other hand, relative positional encodings explicitly encode the relative position.
And so what this means is when you do attention, when you do attention, it explicitly thinks
about whether, you know, a particular embedding is not in position eight, but whether the key,
the query, sorry, whether the key embedding is like minus five from the query embedding or
minus eight from the query embedding.
And the first paper that did this, they just learned relative positional encodings.
And they learned that if I remember correctly, they basically learned a scalar.
So you're centered at zero.
And then you have like, you have like minus and you have minus and plus a certain distance.
And then you also cut this off that might like minus 128 and plus 128 or something like this.
So you have a fixed length vector and anything that's farther away from that gets the same embedding
distance.
The problem with this is number one, it adds learnable parameters.
Number two, it's a little bit more computationally expensive to apply this every time onto your attention
matrix.
And because you need to apply this it everywhere, you need to apply this every time you do attention
the everywhere.
So instead there was a really clever idea called rotary positional encodings and rotary positional
encodings are basically kind of like an absolute positional encoding with the, a lot of the
desirable qualities of relative positional encodings.
And their basic idea was this.
So their basic idea was that they wanted to come up with something where you have an embedding
encoding vector that takes in the actual vector and the position.
